SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The model is specified with clear equations for the GRU encoder/decoder and the selective gate (eqs. 1–4, 8–9) and the training objective is standard maximum likelihood (eq. 18). The experimental protocol uses standard datasets (English Gigaword, DUC 2004, MSR-ATC) and ROUGE metrics, and implementation details (hyperparameters, optimizers, beam search) are provided, which supports the claims. Missing are statistical significance tests and some minor inconsistencies in the description of the sentence representation s (ordering of ~h1 and ~hn), but these do not undermine the technical validity of the approach.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The selective gate that filters encoder hidden states conditioned on a sentence vector is a reasonably novel architectural twist (selective gate network producing h'i from hi and s, eqs. 8–9). However, the paper builds directly on the established seq2seq + attention framework and closely relates to contemporaneous ideas (copy/switch mechanisms cited in Related Work), so the contribution is an incremental architectural variation rather than a radical new paradigm.

CLARITY.justification: Overall organization follows standard flow (model description, selective mechanism, decoder, experiments) and key components are explained with equations and a figure reference. The provided text contains repeated paragraphs, some formatting/noise and small inconsistencies (e.g., repeated blocks in Related Work and slight ambiguity in the concatenation order for s), which make parts harder to follow but do not prevent understanding the core idea.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper evaluates on multiple benchmarks (Gigaword, DUC 2004, MSR-ATC), reports ROUGE metrics, analyzes performance by sentence length, and visualizes the selective gate via first-derivative heat maps, giving empirical support. However, there is no detailed ablation isolating the selective gate versus other design choices (only comparison to s2s+att is mentioned), no reported statistical tests, and some experimental details (e.g., full numeric results in this excerpt) are absent, limiting thoroughness.

IMPACT.justification: Abstractive sentence summarization is an important problem and the selective-encoding idea is practically useful for improving encoder representations prior to decoding; the paper reports improvements over prior baselines and offers interpretable visualizations. Nevertheless, the contribution is incremental and unlikely to alone transform the field, so its impact is moderate.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic (neural abstractive summarization) and methods (seq2seq, attention, GRU, ROUGE evaluation) are squarely within the scope and standards of top NLP/ML venues. The paper uses standard datasets and baselines and is positioned for the NLP community.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares to appropriate recent baselines (ABS, CAs2s, Feats2s, Luong-NMT, etc.) and uses the same Gigaword test set and ROUGE settings to enable fair comparisons. It would be stronger with explicit ablations and significance testing, and the excerpt does not show detailed numeric tables here, but the comparisons described are generally appropriate and relevant.
